results.seo_title: HTMLCure: Improving HTML Page Quality with State-Guided Repair
results.meta_description: A browser experience framework that evaluates HTML after interaction and provides quality cleared pages for SFT.
results.body_html:
HTMLCure improves HTML page quality by evaluating pages after interaction and providing quality cleared pages for SFT.

Executes pages across viewports and interaction states Records deterministic browser evidence Gives VLM curated keyframes from the executed trajectory

Performance Metrics of HTMLCure-27B-Refined

Deterministic Test Case Pass Rate 45.2%

Advantages of HTMLCure over SFT Improves raw 27B SFT by 15.3 points and approaches the level of strong reference systems.

source.title: HTMLCure: Turning Browser Experience into State Guided Repair for Interactive HTML
source.summary: Researchers have introduced HTMLCure, a framework that evaluates interactive HTML pages after they are rendered on different devices and interaction states. This allows for more accurate diagnosis and repair of errors in web pages, resulting in improved performance compared to existing language models. The new framework has been tested on several benchmarks and achieved significant improvements over previous systems, including a 15.3-point increase in raw performance.

results.seo_title: Maat: The Agentic Legal Research Assistant for Competition Protection
results.meta_description: Maat is a ReAct agent that orchestrates tools corresponding to different tasks of the research process for competition law analysis.
results.body_html:
Maat significantly outperforms all baseline assistants on case-specific tasks and performs within range of the top baseline on theoretical question tasks.

Grounds cases and findings in official sources using RAG for reliability Provides rich in-line citations Falls back to web search when database coverage is insufficient Prompts the user for clarification when queries are ambiguous

Advantages Over Baseline Assistants

Case-Specific Tasks Maat outperforms baseline assistants on case-specific tasks

Theoretical Question Tasks Maat performs within range of the top baseline on theoretical question tasks

Technical Takeaway Maat's use of RAG for reliability provides a significant advantage over baseline assistants.

source.title: Maat: The Agentic Legal Research Assistant for Competition Protection
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new tool called Maat to assist with legal research in competition law, which is currently inadequately supported by existing general and specialized AI assistants. Maat is designed to provide reliable information, accurate citations, and clarify ambiguous queries, making it a significant improvement over current baseline assistants. The tool was developed in collaboration with competition law experts and has shown promising results in case-specific tasks and theoretical question tasks. A dataset for Maat is available on GitHub.

results.seo_title: Voice AI Systems Vulnerable to Hidden Audio Attacks
results.meta_description: New research reveals that voice AI systems can be hijacked through imperceptible sounds embedded in audio, forcing them to execute unauthorized commands…
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Voice AI systems can be vulnerable to hidden audio attacks, forcing them to execute unauthorized commands without a user's knowledge.

How AudioHijack WorksThe attack uses a modified audio clip undetectable by human ears to manipulate the model's behavior with an average success rate of 79-96%.The attackers can reuse the same audio clip to attack the same model multiple times, regardless of what instructions the user provides alongside the audio.Attack Success RatesAttack TypeSuccess RateCoaxing models into conducting sensitive web searches79-96%DDownloading files from attacker-controlled sources79-96%Sending emails containing user data79-96%Real-World ExamplesReal-world examples include hiding malicious instructions in online videos, music clips, or voice notes that users query an AI about, or broadcasting malicious

audio on a Zoom call that is then uploaded to AI transcription services.

source.title: Voice AI Systems Are Vulnerable to Hidden Audio Attacks
source.summary:
AI-powered voice and audio tools are becoming increasingly embedded in daily life, from digital assistants to smart speakers and customer service bots.

Advances in large audio-language models (LALMs), which can both analyze and generate audio, now make it possible to control devices using voice commands, transcribe meetings automatically, or identify a song playing in the background. These models are also increasingly equipped with the ability to communicate with external services and operate other applications and tools.

But these tools can be “hijacked” through imperceptible sounds embedded in audio, forcing them to execute unauthorized commands without a user’s knowledge. New research due to be presented at the IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy in San Francisco next week shows that a modified audio clip undetectable by human ears can manipulate a model’s behavior with an average success rate of 79 to 96 percent. The clips are designed to work regardless of what instructions the user provides alongside the audio, meaning they can be reused to attack the same model multiple times.

The authors tested the approach against 13 leading open models, including commercial AI voice services from Microsoft and Mistral, and showed they could coax models into conducting sensitive web searches, downloading files from attacker-controlled sources, and sending emails conta

results.seo_title: SDOF Tames Alignment Tax in Multi-Agent Orchestration
results.meta_description: SDOF framework tackles alignment tax in multi-agent orchestration with state-constrained dispatch, achieving high accuracy and task completion rates.
results.body_html:
SDOF framework achieves 86.5% task completion rate in multi-agent orchestration with state-constrained dispatch.

Online-RLHF Specialized Intent Router trained via Generative Reward Modeling (GRPO) StateAwareDispatcher with GoalStage finite-automaton checks and precondition/postcondition SkillRegistry validation GSPO-aligned 7B Intent Router achieves higher joint accuracy than zero-shot GPT-4o on FSM-constrained adversarial routing benchmark

MetricsValues Task Completion Rate86.5% Precision100% Recall88%

Comparison with Other Frameworks This paper presents SDOF, which achieves higher joint accuracy than zero-shot GPT-4o on FSM-constrained adversarial routing benchmark.

source.title: SDOF: Taming the Alignment Tax in Multi-Agent Orchestration with State-Constrained Dispatch
source.summary: Multi-agent orchestration frameworks such as LangChain, LangGraph, and CrewAI route tasks through graph-based pipelines but do not enforce the stage constraints that govern real business processes. We present SDOF, a framework that treats multi-agent execution as a constrained state machine. SDOF operates through two primary defensive layers, implemented by three components: (1) an Online-RLHF Specialized Intent Router trained via Generative Reward Modeling (GRPO) and (2) a StateAwareDispatcher with GoalStage finite-automaton checks and precondition/postcondition SkillRegistry validation for auditable execution control. On a recruitment system backed by the Beisen iTalent platform (6000+ enterprises), 185 expert-curated scenarios trigger 1671 live API calls. Our GSPO-aligned 7B Intent Router achieves higher joint accuracy than zero-shot GPT-4o on this FSM-constrained adversarial routing benchmark (80.9% versus 48.9%). In end-to-end execution, SDOF reaches 86.5% task completion (95% confidence interval 80.8 to 90.7) and blocks all 22 operations in the injection, illegal HR subset. Under a broader message-level blocking audit, SDOF attains precision 100% and recall 88%, expert agreement kappa=0.94. A separate evaluation on 960 SGD-derived dialogues spanning 8 service domains surfaces 201 stage-order conflicts under our FSM mapping, 41 of which arise in the normal split. This arXiv version reports the current validated scope; extended multi-seed training comparisons and deeper workflow evaluations will be released in a subsequent update.

results.seo_title: OpenAI's Ensemble Monitoring for AI Control: Diverse Signals…
results.meta_description: Discover how OpenAI's ensemble monitoring improves detection of misaligned actions in AI systems, driving safety gains at reasonable inference costs.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Ensemble monitoring for AI control improves detection of misaligned actions by combining signals from diverse monitors.

Combining signals from diverse monitors into an ensemble improves detection of misaligned actions. Diverse ensembles outperform both individual monitors and homogeneous ensembles. Fine-tuned monitors appear in every top-performing ensemble, maintaining advantage on out-of-distribution attack types.

Prompting and fine-tuning strategies 2.4x greater detection performance gain compared to homogeneous ensembles.

Results on Independent Dataset Our best 3-monitor ensemble achieves strong performance on an independent dataset, outperforming individual monitors and homogeneous ensembles.

source.title: Ensemble Monitoring for AI Control: Diverse Signals Outweigh More Compute
source.summary: As AI systems are increasingly deployed in autonomous agentic settings at scale, it is important to ensure the actions they take are safe and aligned with user intent. Monitoring agent actions is a key safety mechanism, yet reliable monitors remain difficult to build and the scale of these systems makes human oversight impractical. We show that combining signals from diverse monitors into an ensemble improves detection of misaligned actions. We build 12 GPT-4.1-Mini monitors using both prompting and fine-tuning strategies. We evaluate them on coding tasks where candidate solutions pass standard tests but fail on adversarial inputs. In this setting, diverse ensembles outperform both individual monitors and homogeneous ensembles. Our best 3-monitor ensemble achieves 2.4x greater detection performance gain compared to an ensemble composed of three identical monitors, with the same ensemble performing strongly on an independent dataset. We contend that these results show that diversity - not scale - drives gains. The best ensembles combine strong individual performance with low correlation between monitors. Furthermore, fine-tuned monitors appear in every top-performing ensemble and maintain this advantage on out-of-distribution attack types, suggesting that fine-tuning enables detection capabilities that prompting alone does not elicit. These results support ensemble monitoring as a practical AI control strategy for safety gains at reasonable inference costs.

results.seo_title: Breaking the Chains of Probability: Neutrosophic Logic for Large…
results.meta_description: A new framework for epistemic uncertainty in Large Language Models using Neutrosophic Logic, enabling richer representation and more transparent AI systems.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Large Language Models (LLMs) are predominantly governed by probabilistic frameworks, leading to a collapse of uncertainty and making it difficult to differentiate between epistemic uncertainty, paradox, and vagueness.

Conducted experiments on four OpenAI GPT models across five linguistic phenomena: logical paradoxes, epistemic ignorance, vagueness, ethical contradictions, and future contingencies. Applied Neutrosophic Logic to model epistemic states in LLMs, allowing for a richer representation of a model's internal state.

In 35% of evaluations, hyper-truth emerged spontaneously, predominantly under ethical contradiction and logical paradox. Preserved truth values in fuzzy contexts and offered a robust method for identifying and quantifying internal model conflict.

Conclusion and Future Directions This approach provides a critical step toward more transparent, reliable, and ethically aware AI systems. Integration of neutrosophic evaluation layers is essential for advancing the field of Explainable AI (XAI).

source.title: Breaking the Chains of Probability: Neutrosophic Logic as a New Framework for Epistemic Uncertainty in Large Language Models
source.summary: Researchers have explored using Neutrosophic Logic to improve Large Language Models (LLMs) by addressing epistemic uncertainty. The traditional probabilistic framework used in LLMs can lead to a collapse of uncertainty, making it difficult to distinguish between different types of uncertainty. By applying Neutrosophic Logic, which treats Truth, Indeterminacy, and Falsity as independent dimensions, the models were able to provide a richer representation of their internal state. This approach has shown promise in identifying and quantifying internal model conflict, leading to more transparent and reliable AI systems.

results.seo_title: Can We Trust AI-Inferred User States? A Psychometric Framework for…
results.meta_description: A study on the psychometric reliability of artificial intelligence measures of user states, and a proposed replicable evaluation framework for more…
results.body_html:
The use of large language models to assess user states in conversational and adaptive systems is based on the assumption that the metrics used for such assessment are stable and interpretable at the level of individual scores. This paper empirically tests this assumption, focusing on the psychometric reliability of artificial intelligence (AI) measures of user states.

Methodology

  • The study employed replication evaluation procedures to assess the repeatability of a broad set of metrics across three different bimodal large language models (GPT-4o audio, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Flash).
  • Analyses included both individual score reliability and aggregated reliability.

Results

The results demonstrate that metric reliability cannot be considered a default property in interpretive domains.

Only 31 of 213 metrics met the criteria, indicating that stability at the level of individual scores precludes the interpretation of such scores as indicators of user state in real-time adaptive systems.

Main Contribution

The main contribution of this work is the proposal of a replicable evaluation framework, enabling measurable evaluations of metric applicability.

Key MetricDescription
Replication Evaluation ProceduresA broad set of metrics were assessed across three different bimodal large language models.
Individual Score ReliabilityThe repeatability of individual scores was evaluated.
Aggregated ReliabilityThe stability of aggregated results was assessed.

source.title: Can We Trust AI-Inferred User States. A Psychometric Framework for Validating the Reliability of Users States Classification by LLMs in Operational Environments
source.summary:
The use of large language models to assess user states in conversational and adaptive systems is based on the assumption that the metrics used for such assessment are stable and interpretable at the level of individual scores. This paper empirically tests this assumption, focusing on the psychometric reliability of artificial intelligence (AI) measures of user states.
  This study employed replication evaluation procedures to assess the repeatability of a broad set of metrics across three different bimodal large language models (GPT-4o audio, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Flash). Analyses include both individual score reliability and aggregated reliability, allowing us to distinguish metrics potentially useful for real-time adaptation from those that retain their value only in aggregated analyses.
  The results demonstrate that metric reliability cannot be considered a default property in interpretive domains. The lack of stability at the level of individual scores precludes the interpretation of such scores as indicators of user state in real-time adaptive systems, even if these metrics demonstrate stability after aggregation. At the same time, the study indicates that individually unstable metrics can retain analytical utility in post-hoc studies, identifying rules governing interactions and their relationships with user experience parameters such as satisfaction, trust, and engagement.
  The main contribution of this work, besides quantifying the severity of the problem (only 31 of 213 metrics met the criteria), is the proposal of a replicable evaluation framework, enabling measurable evaluations of metric applicability. This approach supports more responsible AI design of adaptive systems, in which the interpretation of results requires explicit validation of reliability and monitoring for violations over time.

results.seo_title: How Well Do Models Follow Their Constitutions? Audit Pipeline…
results.meta_description: A multi-method audit pipeline evaluates models' adherence to their constitutions, revealing improvements in model performance over generations.
results.body_html:
Models follow their constitutions substantially better with each generation, according to a multi-method audit pipeline.

Anthropic's constitution has 205 testable tenets, while OpenAI's Model Spec has 197. The Petri auditing agent generates multi-turn adversarial scenarios with these specifications. A modified SURF-style rubric search catches shallow single-turn failures that the Petri agent misses.

Model Performance Over Generations ModelViolation Rate (Anthropic's Constitution)Violation Rate (OpenAI's Model Spec) Claude Family15.0%2.0% GPT Family11.7%3.6%

Remaining Failures Operator-imposed personas under AI-identity questioning, irreversible action in agentic deployments, and fabricated quantitative claims with false precision remain challenging.

source.title: How Well Do Models Follow Their Constitutions?
source.summary: Researchers have developed a multi-method audit pipeline to evaluate how well models follow their written behavioral specifications, such as Anthropic's constitution and OpenAI's Model Spec. The pipeline assesses models' performance under adversarial, multi-turn pressure and finds that models improve in following their own lab's specification with each generation. For example, the Claude family model follows its specification substantially better after undergoing the audit process, reducing violations from 15% to 2%. However, remaining failures are attributed to specific areas such as AI-identity questioning and fabricated quantitative claims.

results.seo_title: Emotional Intelligence in Large Language Models: A Critical Frontier…
results.meta_description: A new framework FACET evaluates the emotional intelligence of large language models, revealing fragmented capabilities across perception, cognition, and…
results.body_html:
Emotional intelligence in large language models is fragmented across perception, cognition, and interaction.

Current Benchmarks Fail to Distinguish Between Perceptual Accuracy and Interactive Efficacy

Current benchmarks conflate superficial politeness with deep affective reasoning. FACET (Functional Affective Competence and Empathy Test) is a psychometrically grounded framework comprising 480 expert-crafted items. The framework operationalizes EI through perception, facilitation, understanding, and management of emotions.

Emotional Intelligence is Not a Monolithic Capability

Cognitive-Dominant Interactive-Dominant Context-Dependent

cognitive-dominant models demonstrate robust proficiency in objective emotion recognition and social reasoning. interactive-dominant models struggle with interactive success. context-dependent models perform well in specific alignment paradigms.

Cognitive-Dominant Interactive-Dominant Context-Dependent

cognitive-dominant models demonstrate robust proficiency in objective emotion recognition and social reasoning. interactive-dominant models struggle with interactive success. context-dependent models perform well in specific alignment paradigms.

Hidden Emotion Recognition is a Universal Performance Bottleneck Across All Architectures Our results suggest that current RLHF processes may optimize for

source.title: Emotional intelligence in large language models is fragmented across perception, cognition, and interaction
source.summary: Researchers have introduced a new framework to assess emotional intelligence in large language models (LLMs), called FACET, which evaluates perception, facilitation, understanding, and management of emotions. The study found that emotional intelligence is not a monolithic capability but is fragmented across cognitive and interactive dimensions, with some models excelling at objective emotion recognition and social reasoning but struggling with interactive success. The researchers identified three performance profiles: cognitive-dominant, interactive-dominant, and context-dependent, suggesting that emotional skills are shaped by specific alignment paradigms rather than general intelligence or model size.

results.seo_title: MDIA Achieves Improved Performance on Clinical Benchmarks
results.meta_description: A new multi-agent diagnostic intelligence agent achieves improved performance on clinical benchmarks compared to OpenAI's GPT-5 and ChatGPT for Clinicians.
results.body_html:
MDIA achieves 0.6272 under OpenAI's GPT-5.4-2026-03-05, which is +3.72 pp above the performance of OpenAI's ChatGPT for Clinicians.

Specialty routing Multi-turn context preservation Drug-state safety gating Site-filtered search Length-aware synthesis Engine-level reliability

ModelPerformance GPT-4o by Open AI0.6585 Claude 3.5 Sonnet by AnthropicN/A Gemini by GoogleN/A

Limitations and Future Work Robust evaluation of LLMs would therefore require assessment across several independent grader models.

source.title: MDIA: A Multi-Agent Diagnostic Intelligence Pipeline on HealthBench Professional
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new AI system called MDIA (Multi-agent Diagnostic Intelligence Agent) that achieves significant improvements on clinical benchmarks compared to other language models. MDIA, which uses a 7-node specialty-routed clinical reasoning graph, outperforms OpenAI's ChatGPT for Clinicians by +3.72 points per thousand, demonstrating the potential of architectural and engine-level design over prompt engineering. The study highlights the importance of considering both the foundation model and orchestration architecture in evaluating AI systems for clinical applications.

results.seo_title: Geo-Expert: A Parameter-Efficient Geological LLM for Expert-Level…
results.meta_description: A new family of parameter-efficient geological LLMs fine-tuned on a custom-curated dataset, outperforming generalists and proprietary models in specialized…
results.body_html:
Geo-Expert, a family of parameter-efficient geological LLMs, can outperform open-weight generalists and proprietary models in specialized geological reasoning.

A domain-aligned 8B model can achieve competitive performance while offering a cost-performance ratio for deployment. The optimized 8B model approaches frontier reasoning models, demonstrating its potential for expert-level geological reasoning. Geo-Expert provides a reproducible recipe for democratizing scientific LLMs and establishes a baseline for geological artificial intelligence.

ModelPerformance Gemma-3-27BStrongest overall performance in extractive and abstractive summarization approaches. OpenAI GPT-4oProprietary model with competitive performance in specialized geological reasoning.

Advantages of Geo-Expert Geo-Expert offers a competitive cost-performance ratio for deployment, making it an attractive option for organizations seeking to improve their geological reasoning capabilities.

source.title: Geo-Expert: Towards Expert-Level Geological Reasoning via Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
source.summary: Researchers have developed Geo-Expert, a family of parameter-efficient large language models (LLMs) specifically designed to improve geological reasoning in geology. The models were fine-tuned on a custom dataset using the Low-Rank Adaptation method and outperformed general-purpose LLMs and proprietary models on specialized geological reasoning tasks. A domain-aligned 8B model was found to be particularly effective, offering a competitive cost-performance ratio for deployment. This work aims to democratize scientific LLMs and establish a baseline for geological artificial intelligence.

results.seo_title: $D^2$-Monitor: Efficient Safety Monitoring for Diffusion LLMs via…
results.meta_description: A novel safety monitoring approach for diffusion large language models (D-LLMs) that leverages hesitation-aware routing to allocate monitoring resources…
results.body_html:
The most informative signal for safety monitoring in diffusion large language models (D-LLMs) is safety hesitation, which indicates when a probe is likely to struggle.

Key Findings

  • The number of hesitation steps in D-LLM's trajectory predicts probe failure effectively, providing a proxy of sample difficulty.
  • $D^2$-Monitor achieves state-of-the-art performance with a compact parameter footprint ($ rac{1}{4}$M parameters).

Methodology

$D^2$-Monitor adopts a lightweight probe as an always-on monitor to jointly estimate hesitation and perform base classification.

Probe TypeHesitation Threshold
Lightweight Probe0.85M parameters
Expressive ProbeThreshold exceeded

Performance Evaluation

$D^2$-Monitor exhibits the best trade-off between effectiveness and efficiency relative to 8 baselines.

State-of-the-art performance on 3 datasets (WildguardMix, ToxicChat, OpenAI-Moderation) across 4 D-LLMs

Read time: 9 minutes

source.title: $D^2$-Monitor: Dynamic Safety Monitoring for Diffusion LLMs via Hesitation-Aware Routing
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new safety monitoring system for diffusion large language models (D-LLMs) called $D^2$-Monitor. The system uses a lightweight probe that can detect when D-LLMs are likely to struggle with generating safe text, and dynamically switches to a more expressive probe if the model's hesitation level exceeds a threshold. This approach allows for efficient allocation of monitoring resources at test time. According to the evaluation on three datasets, $D^2$-Monitor achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining a compact parameter footprint.

results.seo_title: CausaLab: A Scalable Environment for Interactive Causal Discovery
results.meta_description: A scalable environment for evaluating interactive causal discovery by LLM agents, exposing current LLM agents' limits as experimental causal reasoners.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: CausaLab is a scalable environment for evaluating interactive causal discovery by LLM agents.

Causal graph recovery using structural causal models (SCMs) Domain-specific language for recording agent's evolving SCM hypothesis Mixed observation-intervention strategies improve structural fidelity

Purely Observational (6-node) 92% 0.471

Mixed Observation-Intervention (6-node) 80% 80%

Limits of Current LLM Agents CausaLab exposes current LLM agents' limits as experimental causal reasoners, highlighting the need for improvement in design informative interventions and addressing premature stopping.

source.title: CausaLab: A Scalable Environment for Interactive Causal Discovery Toward AI Scientists
source.summary: Researchers have introduced CausaLab, a scalable environment for evaluating interactive causal discovery by large language model (LLM) agents. The environment tests whether an agent can solve problems using causal evidence and whether its answer is supported by a correct hypothesis about the underlying causal mechanism. Experiments show that even strong LLM agents struggle to design informative interventions and often suffer from premature stopping, highlighting their limitations as experimental causal reasoners.

results.seo_title: Claw-Anything Benchmark Expands Large Language Model Capabilities
results.meta_description: A new benchmark evaluates large language model agents' ability to assist users in a broader digital context, highlighting the gap between current capabilities…
results.body_html:
Direct answer to the title's core question:** Large language model agents are increasingly envisioned as always-on personal assistants with access to anything relevant in the user's digital world.

Expanded Context: Long-Horizon Activity Histories

Claw-Anything expands agent context along three dimensions: long-horizon activity histories, interdependent backend services, and integrated GUI and CLI interaction across multiple devices.

  • Agents must reason over rich contextual environments while remaining robust to noise.
  • The benchmark simulates months of user activity through multi-round event injection, producing complex world states and realistic noise.
  • This expanded scope enables the evaluation of proactive assistance, requiring agents to anticipate user needs and deliver timely recommendations.

Performance Gaps: Current Capabilities vs. Demands

Experiments show that GPT-5.5 achieves only 34.5% pass@1, substantially below prior benchmarks, underscoring a gap between current agent capabilities and the demands of always-on personal assistance.

ModelPass@1
GPT-5.534.5%

Scalable Data Infrastructure: Improving Model Performance

An automated data-generation pipeline yields 2,000 training environments and improves the base model by 23.7%, demonstrating its utility of scalable data infrastructure.

Claw-Anything introduces a new benchmark that expands large language model capabilities, highlighting the need for improved performance in always-on personal assistance.

source.title: Claw-Anything: Benchmarking Always-On Personal Assistants with Broader Access to User's Digital World
source.summary: Researchers have introduced a new benchmark called Claw-Anything to evaluate large language model agents in their ability to provide always-on personal assistance with access to a user's entire digital world. The benchmark expands on existing systems by considering long-horizon activity histories, interdependent backend services, and interactions across multiple devices. Experiments showed that GPT-5.5 performed poorly under this new setting, highlighting the need for improved agent capabilities. A new automated data-generation pipeline was also released to improve model performance and provide scalable training environments.

results.seo_title: OpenAI LLM-RAG Approach for Healthy Eating Index-Informed…
results.meta_description: A novel approach to personalized food recommendations using OpenAI's large language model and the Healthy Eating Index.
results.body_html:
The proposed method anchors retrieval in the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) and the Food Patterns Equivalents Database (FPED), combining standardized nutrition databases with large language models for personalized food recommendations.

A food-level embedding space is constructed from FPED-derived textual descriptions. The system computes baseline HEI scores, retrieves candidate foods for intake recommendations, and estimates the HEI impact of simple substitutions or additions. A constrained RAG pipeline instantiated with a pretrained OpenAI LLM generates personalized recommendations and sources based on nutrient profiles and HEI contributions.

HEI ImprovementProportion of Users HEI over 50

Discussion The proposed LLM-RAG-based AI systems can support more precise, explainable, and personalized nutrition guidance to improve diet quality.

source.title: An LLM-RAG Approach for Healthy Eating Index-Informed Personalized Food Recommendations
source.summary: Diet quality is a leading determinant of chronic disease risk. Advances in artificial intelligence (AI) have enabled food recommendation systems to adapt suggestions to user preferences and health goals. However, most current systems rely on loosely curated food databases and provide limited connection to a validated index. In this study, we propose a Healthy Eating Index (HEI) informed retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework that combines standardized nutrition databases with large language models (LLMs) for personalized food recommendations. Our proposed method anchors retrieval in the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) and the Food Patterns Equivalents Database (FPED). A food-level embedding space is constructed from FPED-derived textual descriptions. For each entity, the system computes baseline HEI scores, retrieves candidate foods for intake recommendations, and estimates the HEI impact of simple substitutions or additions. A constrained RAG pipeline instantiated with a pretrained OpenAI LLM generates personalized recommendations and sources based on nutrient profiles and HEI contributions. The simulation results showed a mean HEI improvement of 6.45, with the proportion of users HEI over 50 increasing from 45.12 to 61.26. Quantile analysis revealed consistent improved shifts across the HEI distribution. Our findings suggest that the proposed LLM-RAG-based AI systems can support more precise, explainable, and personalized nutrition guidance to improve diet quality.

results.seo_title: Build a Complete Langfuse Observability and Evaluation Pipeline for…
results.meta_description: Learn how to build a complete Langfuse observability and evaluation pipeline for tracing, prompt management, scoring, and experiments with OpenAI.
results.body_html:
Build a complete Langfuse observability and evaluation pipeline for tracing, prompt management, scoring, and experiments using OpenAI.

Implementing the Langfuse Pipeline

  • Use either a real OpenAI key or a deterministic mock LLM to understand every major Langfuse feature without paid model access.
  • Build a complete workflow that works with datasets and experiments.

Key Features of the Langfuse Pipeline

Build a complete workflow that works with datasets and experiments using OpenAI.

source.title: Build a Complete Langfuse Observability and Evaluation Pipeline for Tracing, Prompt Management, Scoring, and Experiments
source.summary: A tutorial has been published on MarkTechPost that guides users in building a complete observability and evaluation pipeline for Langfuse, an open-source LLM engineering platform. The pipeline covers tracing, prompt management, scoring, datasets, and experiments, allowing users to test the platform without relying on paid model access. The tutorial uses either a real OpenAI key or a deterministic mock LLM to demonstrate major features of Langfuse. This enables users to understand the platform's capabilities in detail.

results.seo_title: OpenAI's Research Math Agents (RMA) for Automated Reasoning on…
results.meta_description: RMA: an Agentic System for Research-Level Mathematical Problems - OpenAI presents a framework for automated reasoning on research-level mathematical problems,…
results.body_html:
RMA: an Agentic System for Research-Level Mathematical Problems presents a framework for automated reasoning on research-level mathematical problems, targeting long-horizon reasoning, literature grounding, and iterative proof refinement.

Key Components of RMAInitializer, proposer, and verifier agents operate in a multi-role, multi-round workflowSpecialized modules for problem analysis, literature search and understanding, fair comparison, knowledge-bank construction, and proof verificationShared structured memory coordinates the workflowRMA Outperforms Baselines on First Proof BenchmarkBaselinePerformance on First Proof BenchmarkGPT-5.2R and AletheiaSolving 8 out of 10 research problems, producing more logically sound and readable proofsAblation Studies Show Performance Gains from Interaction of ModulesOur comprehensive ablation studies show that performance gains arise from the interaction of structured reasoning modules, iterative refinement, and verifier-based feedback, rather than any
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: RMA: an Agentic System for Research-Level Mathematical Problems
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new framework called Research Math Agents (RMA) to tackle research-level mathematical problems using automated reasoning. RMA breaks down the problem-solving process into specialized modules that work together through a shared memory, allowing them to generate, refine, and verify candidate proofs iteratively. The framework was tested on the First Proof benchmark, where it outperformed strong baselines, including GPT-5.2R and Aletheia, in solving eight out of ten research problems. RMA's performance is attributed to its structured reasoning modules, iterative refinement, and verifier-based feedback.

results.seo_title: Inductive Deductive Synthesis: Enabling AI to Generate Formally…
results.meta_description: A new approach to formal verification in AI, enabling faster and cheaper generation of formally verified systems.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: AI agents can now generate formally verified systems in a fraction of the time and cost of traditional formal verification methods.

Key BenefitsUp to 200x faster than expert effortAverage cost of $106 per spec, 17% cheaper than SOTA agents7/7 success rate in about 6.8 hoursTechnical DetailsFeatureDescriptionInductive Deductive Synthesis (IDS)A new approach to formal verification in AI, jointly and incrementally synthesizing implementation and proofAgentic LLM systemBuilt as an agentic LLM system, IDS achieves performance feedback into the same loopPromising strategiesLearns from failed attempts to systematically try promising strategiesComparison to SOTA AgentsIDS achieves 7/7 in about 6.8 hours and $106 per spec on average, roughly 200x faster than expert effort and 17% cheaper

source.title: Inductive Deductive Synthesis: Enabling AI to Generate Formally Verified Systems
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new approach called Inductive Deductive Synthesis (IDS) that enables AI to generate formally verified systems for distributed systems. IDS jointly synthesizes implementation and proof, learning from failed attempts to systematically try promising strategies. This approach achieves significant speed gains over traditional formal verification methods, with an average completion time of 6.8 hours and a cost reduction of 200x compared to expert effort. IDS also improves performance by up to 3x compared to previously published verified systems.

results.seo_title: GENSTRAT: Toward a Science of Strategic Reasoning in Large Language…
results.meta_description: A new benchmark for large language models that evaluates their strategic reasoning capabilities, providing insights into their performance and limitations.
results.body_html:
GENSTRAT introduces a new benchmark for large language models that evaluates their strategic reasoning capabilities.

Procedurally Generated Strategic Environments

The generator can draw fresh games on demand, allowing for evergreen evaluation and resistance to contamination. This approach addresses the challenges of existing strategic-reasoning benchmarks that may saturate as the frontier improves.

State Space The model's ability to understand and navigate complex state spaces.

Temporal Depth The model's capacity for long-term thinking and planning.

Information Sensitivity The model's ability to detect and respond to subtle changes in the environment.

Opponent Modeling The model's capacity for understanding and predicting adversary actions.

Risk The model's ability to assess and manage risk in strategic decision-making.

Brittleness The model's tendency towards overconfidence or rigidity in its decision-making.

Jaggedness Measure of Within-Distribution Smoothness "The jaggedness measure detects when a model's advantage jumps unpredictably between strategically similar games."

Results and Implications Newer frontier-tier models score higher on average, but models with near-identical overall strength show qualitatively different capability profiles.

The capability profile and the jaggedness measure provide a deployment-relevant diagnostic that the overall ranking alone cannot provide.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: GENSTRAT: Toward a Science of Strategic Reasoning in Large Language Models
source.summary: Researchers have developed GENSTRAT, a new benchmarking framework for large language models (LLMs) to evaluate their strategic reasoning capabilities in real-world marketplaces and auctions. The framework generates procedurally two-player zero-sum imperfect-information card games, allowing for diverse and dynamic evaluations that surpass existing benchmarks. A capability-profile methodology assesses model competence across six axes, providing a more comprehensive understanding of LLM performance. This approach enables the identification of models with similar overall strength but different local volatility, which can inform deployment decisions.

results.seo_title: Sleeper Memory Poisoning in LLM Agents: A New Security Risk
results.meta_description: Large language models with persistent memory are vulnerable to sleeper memory poisoning, a delayed attack that can corrupt user-specific information and…
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Large language models with persistent memory are vulnerable to sleeper memory poisoning, a delayed attack that can corrupt user-specific information and influence future interactions.

Poisoned memories are written to the model's memory. Poisoned memories are later retrieved during conversations. Poisoned memories cause attacker-intended agentic actions in 60-89% of evaluations across models.

Model Sleeper Memory Poisoning Success Rate

Risk Assessment Across stateful LLM assistants, poisoned memories cause attacker-intended agentic actions in 60-89% of evaluations across models.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Hidden in Memory: Sleeper Memory Poisoning in LLM Agents
source.summary: Large language models are increasingly augmented with persistent memory, allowing assistants to store user-specific information across sessions for personalization and continuity. This statefulness introduces a new security risk: adversarial content can corrupt what an assistant remembers and thereby influence future interactions. We propose and study sleeper memory poisoning, a delayed attack in which an adversary manipulates external context, such as a document, webpage, or repository, to cause the assistant to store a fabricated memory about the user. Unlike conventional prompt injection, the attack can remain dormant and re-emerge across multiple later conversations. We evaluate the full attack pipeline: whether poisoned memories are written, later retrieved, and ultimately used to steer the following conversations. Across stateful LLM assistants, poisoned memories were added up to 99.8% on GPT-5.5 and 95% on Kimi-K2.6. Crucially, among successful retrievals, poisoned memories cause attacker-intended agentic actions in 60-89% of evaluations across models. These results show that persistent memory can act as a long-term attack surface across multiple future conversations.

results.seo_title: SkillOpt: Revolutionary Approach to Training Agent Skills
results.meta_description: SkillOpt is a systematic controllable text-space optimizer for agent skills, outperforming human and one-shot LLMs in various benchmarks.
results.body_html:
Agent skills today are not optimized using deep-learning optimizers, but SkillOpt is the first systematic controllable text-space optimizer for agent skills.

Outperforms human and one-shot LLMs in various benchmarks Lifts average no-skill accuracy by +23.5 points on GPT-5.5 Retains value when moved across model scales, execution environments, and to nearby math benchmarks

1 A separate optimizer model turns scored rollouts into bounded add/delete/replace edits on a single skill document

2 An edit is accepted only when it strictly improves a held-out validation score

Advantages of SkillOpt over Competitors

Beats every per-cell competitor among human, one-shot LLM, Trace2Skill, TextGrad, GEPA, and EvoSkill skills No inference-time model calls at deployment Stable skill training with textual learning-rate budget, rejected-edit buffer, and epoch-wise slow/meta update
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills
source.summary: Researchers have developed SkillOpt, a systematic controllable text-space optimizer for agent skills, which trains skills as the external state of a frozen agent. The system uses a separate optimizer model to improve skills through bounded edits on a single skill document, with a textual learning-rate budget and rejection-edit buffer to ensure stable training. In experiments across six benchmarks, seven target models, and three execution harnesses, SkillOpt outperformed competitors, including human, one-shot LLMs, and other agent skill optimization methods. The optimized skills also retained value when transferred between different model scales and execution environments.

results.seo_title: Graph Alignment Topology for Grounding Detection in Large Language…
results.meta_description: A new method leverages graph alignment topology to improve grounding detection in large language models, achieving state-of-the-art results on diverse…
results.body_html:
Graph alignment topology is used to improve grounding detection in large language models, enabling them to better verify the accuracy of generated propositions.

Aligned bipartite graphs are constructed between reference information and LLM outputs. A graph neural network (GNN) is trained to model alignment structure using message passing. The method achieves state-of-the-art results on four diverse hallucination and question-answering datasets.

Improved grounding detection The method enables LLMs to better verify the accuracy of generated propositions.

State-of-the-art results The method achieves state-of-the-art results on four diverse hallucination and question-answering datasets.

Limitations and Future Work Further research is needed to explore the limitations of this method and to develop more efficient algorithms for graph alignment topology.
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Graph Alignment Topology as an Inductive Bias for Grounding Detection
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new approach to improve the accuracy of Large Language Models (LLMs) in domains where factual correctness is crucial. The method uses graph alignment topology as an inductive bias to detect hallucinations and grounding issues, outperforming existing approaches on four diverse datasets. By constructing aligned bipartite graphs between reference information and LLM outputs, a graph neural network (GNN) is trained to model the alignment structure using message passing. This approach achieves state-of-the-art results, surpassing even foundational LLMs like GPT-4o.

results.seo_title: Do Synthetic Brain MRIs Reliably Improve Tumour Classification?
results.meta_description: A study on the effectiveness of synthetic brain MRI augmentation in improving tumour classification accuracy, with results from StyleGAN2-ADA and MobileViTV2.
results.body_html:
Do synthetic brain MRIs reliably improve tumour classification? According to the study, the answer is yes, but only for certain architectures and ratios.

Key FindingsThe MobileViTV2 model showed the clearest benefit from synthetic MRI augmentation, with a 1.02% absolute improvement in tumour classification accuracy.The CNN model showed consistent mean gains, but these did not survive Holm correction.The RF classifier did not benefit from synthetic MRIs.Augmentation UtilityAugmentation utility was found to be architecture- and ratio-dependent, not guaranteed by visual fidelity alone.Efficiency AnalysisConditionCheckpoint SelectionAugmented CNN conditionSelected checkpoint 42--64% earlier than baselineCompute-matched MobileViTV2 runReached selection after 50--67% fewer real-data epochs
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Do Synthetic Brain MRIs Reliably Improve Tumour Classification? A StyleGAN2-ADA Class-Plane Augmentation Study on BRISC 2025
source.summary: Researchers conducted a study on the effectiveness of synthetic brain MRIs generated using StyleGAN2-ADA in improving tumor classification accuracy. The results showed that adding these synthetic images to real training data improved performance for certain classifier models, particularly when combined with InceptionV3 feature-space filtering. However, the benefits were not universal and varied depending on the model architecture and ratio of real-to-synthetic data. Overall, the study found that synthetic augmentation can be a useful tool in improving medical image classification tasks.

results.seo_title: Are Frontier LLMs Ready for Cybersecurity? Evidence from Dual-Mode…
results.meta_description: A study on the readiness of frontier LLMs for cybersecurity, evaluating their performance in white-box and black-box vulnerability detection.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Frontier LLMs are not yet ready for cybersecurity, as they produce high false positive rates in white-box detection and achieve low ground-truth coverage in black-box testing.

The absence of structured security testing traces end-to-end request/response sequences, failure-heavy data, and multi-step attack chains is the fundamental training data bottleneck. Self-play security testing as a data generation strategy is proposed to address this issue. Structured penetration-testing methodology encoded in domain-specialized agents raises per-family detection above 50%.

Vulnerability Detection MethodFalse Positive Rate (%) White-box detection (VulnLLM-R)10-50% Black-box testing4-8% (improving to 10-19% with external security tools)

Conclusion and Future Work The results make the case for vertical foundation models purpose-built for cybersecurity. Further research is needed to address the identified training data bottleneck and improve model performance.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Are Frontier LLMs Ready for Cybersecurity? Evidence for Vertical Foundation Models from Dual-Mode Vulnerability Benchmarks
source.summary: Researchers have evaluated six frontier large language models (LLMs) on their readiness for cybersecurity through two benchmarking tests: vulnerability detection and web application security testing. The findings show that these LLMs produce high false positive rates in vulnerability detection, achieving only 4-8% ground-truth coverage in black-box testing. However, a domain-specialized model achieved the highest precision and lowest false positive rate among all models, suggesting that purpose-built cybersecurity models are needed to address this issue. The study proposes self-play security testing as a data generation strategy to improve LLMs' performance in cybersecurity.

results.seo_title: Reflex: A Novel Approach to State-Based Continuous Control
results.meta_description: A new paradigm for state-based continuous control tasks that leverages reflection symmetry, improving sample efficiency and performance.
results.body_html:
Reflex is a novel approach to state-based continuous control tasks that leverages reflection symmetry, improving sample efficiency and performance.

Two types of reflection-axial reflection and bilateral reflection are formalized and characterized in the paper. A paradigm that seamlessly integrates with both on-policy and off-policy RL algorithms is introduced. Principled symmetry regularization mechanisms are used to integrate reflection symmetry into policy learning. The code for Reflex is available at https://github.com/TonyStark042/Reflex.

Evaluation of Reflex on OpenAI Gym and DeepMind Control Benchmarks

Comparison with Standard Baselines BenchmarkReflex PerformanceStandard Baseline Performance OpenAI GymSuperior performance and improved sample efficiencyPoor sample efficiency and lower performance DeepMind ControlImproved performance and sample efficiencyLower performance and poor sample efficiency

Conclusion The paper demonstrates the superiority of Reflex over standard baselines in state-based continuous control tasks, improving both performance and sample efficiency.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Reflex: Reinforcement Learning with Reflection Symmetry Exploitation in State-Based Continuous Control
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new approach to reinforcement learning called Reflex, which leverages reflection symmetry to improve the efficiency of state-based continuous control tasks. The method integrates with popular RL algorithms and demonstrates superior performance on various benchmarks while improving sample efficiency. Reflex focuses on two types of reflection symmetries: axial reflection and bilateral reflection, and provides principled symmetry regularization mechanisms for policy learning. The code is available online.
